# PostGraphile

**What it is:** Instant GraphQL API generated by introspecting a Postgres schema, with a "smart comments/tags" mechanism for declaring metadata the schema itself can't express.
**Axis:** semantic layer.
**Depth:** medium — official docs (v4 legacy + v5/next) only.

## Products & surfaces

| Product | What it is |
|---|---|
| **PostGraphile core** | Schema introspection → GraphQL schema generation, resolvers pushed down to SQL. |
| **Smart tags / smart comments** | SQL `COMMENT ON` metadata (`@foreignKey`, `@primaryKey`, `@unique`, `@behavior`, etc.) that alters the generated GraphQL schema without altering the database schema. |
| **`@behavior`** | v5's unified, composable capability-toggle system, replacing most of v4's individual smart tags. |

## Notable features

| Feature | What it does | Steal? |
|---|---|---|
| Virtual `@foreignKey`/`@primaryKey`/`@unique` | Declares relationships/constraints that don't physically exist in the schema (views, composite types, FDWs) | yes |
| `@behavior +/-token` | One composable grammar for every capability (insert/delete/filter/sort/etc.), additive or subtractive per token | yes |
| Smart comments as the metadata channel | Metadata lives in `COMMENT ON`, since Postgres has no other place to attach it | maybe (mechanism is a workaround, not an ideal) |

## Worth stealing

### Virtual constraints let non-table things behave like tables

Views, materialized views, and composite types can't hold real Postgres constraints (no `PRIMARY KEY`/`FOREIGN KEY`/`UNIQUE` on a view). PostGraphile's `@primaryKey`, `@foreignKey`, and `@unique` smart tags declare **fake constraints that PostGraphile trusts without verifying** — "we do not check it's unique — we trust you" — purely to let the GraphQL layer generate the same relationship traversal, node-identity, and connection machinery it would for a real table. `@foreignKey (col1, ...) references [schema.]table [(col1, ...)]` mirrors real FK syntax exactly, so a view backed by a complex query, or a foreign-data-wrapper table with no enforced constraints, gets full relational GraphQL ergonomics (nested queries, connections) anyway.

### `@behavior` — one composable vocabulary that replaced a pile of bespoke flags

PostGraphile v5 replaced a set of separately-invented smart tags (`@omit`, `@sortable`, `@filterable`, `@simpleCollections`, ...) with a single **composable token grammar**: `@behavior +filter -insert -delete +sort` — each token independently added (`+`) or removed (`-`), on any entity (table, column, function, relation). E.g. `@behavior -insert -delete +filter +sort` on one entity, read as a sentence, says exactly what capabilities it has. The v4→v5 migration docs are explicit that this is a *replacement*, not an addition: `@filterable`/`@sortable`/`@omit` are deprecated in favor of the equivalent `@behavior` tokens. **The lesson: a pile of independently-invented boolean flags (is this sortable? filterable? omittable? from what operations?) eventually collapses into one small composable vocabulary once enough flags accumulate that their combinations start needing explaining.** Worth noting as a shape to design toward from the start, rather than backing into after the flag pile gets unwieldy (as PostGraphile itself did).

## Worth avoiding / caveats

**Smart comments are a hack born of Postgres having nowhere else to put this metadata.** `COMMENT ON TABLE ... IS '@foreignKey (post_id) references posts (id)|@fieldName yourNameHere'` is genuinely a SQL comment string, parsed by PostGraphile at introspection time — there is no dedicated Postgres catalog for "GraphQL-relevant relationship metadata that isn't a real constraint." The mechanism works, but it means the metadata (a) is invisible to any tool that doesn't know to parse `COMMENT ON` bodies as a mini-DSL, (b) has no syntax validation from Postgres itself (a malformed smart comment just silently fails to produce the intended tag), and (c) is easy to lose track of since it's not colocated with a migration's actual DDL in the way a real constraint is. Any system that wants a similar "declare relationships beyond what the physical schema encodes" capability should give that metadata a first-class store, not reach for comment-string parsing as the first move.

## Facts & figures

- `@behavior` system introduced in PostGraphile v5 as a v4-smart-tag replacement (per official migration docs).
